request: We'd like managers to approve employee leave requests, with an escalation if the manager is away.

problem.value: Leave requests stall or get delayed when the approving manager is unavailable, hurting employee experience and compliance with response-time expectations.
problem.evidence: 'approve employee leave requests, with an escalation if the manager is away' — the escalation ask implies today requests can get stuck.

current_process.value: Absence management already exists on the platform per context; likely a single-level manager approval with no fallback path today.
current_process.evidence: Context: 'Major features: absence management, contracts...' — feature exists but escalation is new ask.

actors.value: Employee (requester), direct manager (primary approver); escalation target unspecified — could be skip-level manager, HR, or a designated backup.
actors.evidence: 'managers to approve' and 'escalation if manager is away' name two roles but not the escalation target.

business_objects.value: Leave/Absence request tied to Employee, likely with a leave-balance/quota check per the platform's standard pattern.
business_objects.evidence: Context: 'approval' almost always hides a balance/quota check + multi-level circuit.

business_rules.value: Undefined: how 'away' is detected (declared OOO, no response within X days, on leave themselves), whether balance/quota is checked, whether rule varies by team/country.
business_rules.evidence: Core of the request but mechanism not specified; context flags this as a recurring trap.

workflow.value: Likely states: submitted -> pending manager approval -> (escalated) -> approved/rejected. Trigger for escalation (timeout vs manual vs manager-on-leave event) not defined; sync/async behavior unclear.
workflow.evidence: 'escalation if the manager is away' implies a state transition but not its trigger or timing.

integrations.value: Notification to manager on submission, and to escalation target on escalation; channel (email/in-app) not specified.
integrations.evidence: Standard pattern for approval circuits per context; not detailed in request.

permissions.value: Manager approves direct reports; escalation target needs equivalent rights on the escalated request. Unclear if original manager retains ability to act after escalation.
permissions.evidence: Context: 'approval circuits and permission rules are shared across modules'; 'permissions are forgotten until acceptance testing'.

config_vs_custom.value: Unclear whether escalation rules (who, after how long) are global, or configurable per client/team/country.
config_vs_custom.evidence: Context: 'Client-specific: approval circuits, business rules... rights' — platform is multi-client configurable.

constraints.value: French labor law governs paid leave/RTT; GDPR applies to HR data. No new explicit deadline or budget stated.
constraints.evidence: Context: 'Regulatory: French labor law (paid leave, RTT), GDPR on HR data'.

edge_cases.value: Unaddressed: manager away for an extended period with multiple pending requests, escalation target also away (chained escalation), retroactive/cancelled requests during escalation.

reporting.value: No audit/traceability requirement named yet beyond standard HR record-keeping.
reporting.evidence: Not mentioned in request; no explicit compliance/audit driver raised.

risks.value: Risk of double-approval or requests falling through the cracks if escalation logic and permission handoff aren't precisely defined.
risks.evidence: Inferred from context traps around triggers and permissions.

questions.q: What exactly triggers the escalation — the manager explicitly marking themselves unavailable/on leave, no response within a set number of days, or a manual reassignment by HR?
questions.why: uncertainty x impact: this is the crux of the feature (high impact, currently empty) — it decides whether this is a simple flag check or a timed/event-driven workflow, and changes the entire build.

questions.q: Who is the escalation approver — the manager's own manager (skip-level), HR, or a backup the manager designates themselves? Can it vary by team/country/client?
questions.why: uncertainty x impact: escalation target is undefined (high impact); the platform is multi-client and rules commonly vary by country/client, so this shapes both workflow and permissions.

questions.q: Is the escalation time-based (e.g., auto-escalate after 3 days of no action) or event-based (only when the manager is marked away), and should the original manager still be able to approve after escalation fires?
questions.why: uncertainty x impact: sync vs async trigger and race conditions (two people able to act) are a classic source of rework if undefined upfront (high impact, currently empty).

questions.q: Should leave-balance/quota still be validated before/at approval, and does that check happen before routing to the manager or before escalation?
questions.why: uncertainty x impact: approval flows on this platform almost always hide a quota check; missing it here would require rework later (high impact, unconfirmed).

questions.q: Should the escalation policy (who to escalate to, after how long) be configurable per team/country/client, or is one global rule enough for now?
questions.why: uncertainty x impact: platform is configurable multi-client; a global-only rule vs. per-client customization is a materially different build (high impact, currently empty).

summary.objective: Let managers approve employee leave requests, with a fallback approver stepping in when the manager is unavailable, so requests are never stuck waiting.
summary.scope: Likely extends the existing absence/leave approval flow with: an 'away' detection mechanism, an escalation step to a designated approver, notifications to the right people at each step, and permission handling so the escalation target can act on the request. Probably still includes the standard leave-balance/quota check already implied by the platform's approval pattern.
assumptions: Current process is a single-level manager approval with no existing fallback path.
Escalation target is likely a skip-level manager or HR, to be confirmed.
Standard leave-balance/quota check remains part of the approval flow.
No new explicit regulatory or deadline constraint beyond existing French labor law / GDPR baseline.
summary.blind_spot: The request names 'escalation if the manager is away' but not what 'away' means operationally — a declared out-of-office flag, a timeout with no response, or a manual HR override — nor who the escalation lands on. This single undefined trigger decides whether the build is a simple status check or a timed, asynchronous workflow with race conditions (both manager and escalation target able to act), and it also determines what permission and notification changes ripple outward.

problem.value: Avoid leave requests stalling (and employees being blocked or bypassing approval) when the manager is unavailable to act.
problem.evidence: Client explicitly asks for escalation 'if the manager is away' — implies a known pain point of unactioned requests.

current_process.evidence: Not stated whether an approval flow already exists (manual, email, another module) or this is a net-new feature.

success_metrics.evidence: No target stated (e.g. max delay before escalation, % requests resolved on time).

actors.value: Employee (requester), Manager (primary approver), escalation target unspecified — likely manager's manager, HR, or a designated delegate.
actors.evidence: 'Manager' and 'employee' explicit; escalation recipient not named.

business_objects.value: Leave/Absence request, linked to employee's leave balance/quota; approval circuit metadata.
business_objects.evidence: Standard entities per HR context; not explicitly detailed by client.

business_rules.value: Approval requires manager action; escalation triggers when manager is 'away' — exact detection (out-of-office flag, delegate set, timeout with no response) not specified. Balance/quota check before approval assumed but unconfirmed.
business_rules.evidence: Context: 'approval' almost always hides a balance/quota check + multi-level circuit; 'away' is undefined in the request.

workflow.value: Likely states: submitted → pending manager approval → (escalated) → approved/rejected. Trigger and timing for the escalation transition are unclear.
workflow.evidence: Escalation implies a state change but the trigger (manual vs automatic, delay length) isn't specified.

integrations.value: Notification to manager on submission; notification to escalation target when triggered; possibly calendar/HRIS sync.
integrations.evidence: Standard pattern for approval circuits per context, not explicitly requested.

permissions.evidence: Unclear whether the escalation approver has full approve/reject rights, view-only override, or a different scope than the original manager.

config_vs_custom.evidence: Escalation rules and 'away' detection may vary by team, department, or country (French labor law specifics) — not addressed.

constraints.evidence: No deadline, regulatory reference, or dependency mentioned beyond implicit French labor law (paid leave rules).

edge_cases.evidence: Not addressed: what if the escalation target is also away, multiple managers, retroactive requests, or manager returns mid-escalation.

reporting.evidence: No audit/compliance need explicitly named beyond standard HR data handling (GDPR baseline).

risks.evidence: Rollout and failure risks (e.g. double approval, missed escalation) not discussed.

questions.q: How is a manager's 'away' status determined — an explicit out-of-office flag/calendar sync, a fixed delay with no response (e.g. 48h), or a manually designated delegate?
questions.why: high impact × high uncertainty: this single rule decides whether escalation is automatic/timed (async, needs a scheduler and pending-state UX) or manual/flag-based — completely different build.

questions.q: Who is the escalation target — the manager's own manager, HR, a named delegate per team, and can this vary by department or country?
questions.why: high impact × high uncertainty: determines org-chart lookup logic, permission scope, and whether the rule needs per-client/team configuration.

questions.q: Does the escalation approver get full approve/reject rights identical to the original manager, or a restricted/notify-only role?
questions.why: high impact × empty: permissions are frequently forgotten until acceptance testing per context; this defines what the escalation UI must expose.

questions.q: Is there an existing leave approval flow today (even informal/email-based), or is this the first structured approval circuit for leave?
questions.why: high impact × empty: determines whether we're building a circuit from scratch or extending/replacing an existing mechanism, which changes migration and scope.

questions.q: Should escalation rules (delay threshold, target role) be configurable per team/department/country, or is one fixed rule acceptable for all?
questions.why: high impact × empty: on a multi-client configurable platform, hardcoding a rule that later needs to vary is expensive to retrofit.

summary.objective: Ensure employee leave requests are approved by their manager without stalling indefinitely, by automatically escalating to another approver when the manager is unavailable.
summary.scope: Likely includes: a leave request lifecycle (submitted → pending manager approval → escalated → approved/rejected), a rule detecting manager unavailability, an escalation notification/approval step to a designated backup approver, and balance/quota validation typical of leave approval per HR domain norms.
assumptions: An escalation target exists in the org structure (manager's manager, HR, or a delegate) though not yet named.
A leave/absence approval circuit is being newly formalized rather than replacing a fully-built existing one.
The escalation approver will have similar approve/reject rights to the original manager.
Standard balance/quota check applies before approval, consistent with platform norms.
'Away' detection and escalation delay are not yet defined and may need to be configurable.
summary.blind_spot: The definition of 'manager is away' is undefined — is it a calendar/out-of-office signal, a fixed timeout, or a manual delegate designation? This single ambiguity determines whether the feature is a synchronous flag check or an asynchronous timed/queued escalation with pending-state UX, retries, and notification timing — a fundamentally different build with different failure modes (e.g., escalation firing while manager was mid-approval, or never firing if 'away' isn't properly signaled).

problem.value: Leave requests currently stall or get delayed when the direct manager is unavailable to approve, risking late/blocked leave for employees.
problem.evidence: Client asks for approval + escalation if manager is away — implies a pain point around delay/bottleneck, not stated directly.

current_process.value: Unknown whether an approval circuit for leave already exists in the platform or this is a new flow.
current_process.evidence: Context: 'absence management' is already a major feature of the platform — an approval mechanism may partially exist.

success_metrics.value: Not stated — e.g. no leave request pending > X days, no manual chasing of managers.

actors.value: Employee (requester), Manager (primary approver), and an unspecified escalation approver (skip-level manager, HR, or delegate).
actors.evidence: Client names 'manager' and 'escalation' but not who receives the escalation.

business_objects.value: Leave/Absence request, Employee, Manager, (new) Escalation/Delegate reference.
business_objects.evidence: Context lists Employee, Leave/Absence as existing entities.

business_rules.value: Undefined: how 'manager away' is detected (their own leave status, out-of-office flag, manual delegation, or timeout with no response), whether a balance/quota check gates approval, and whether escalation rules vary by client/country/contract.
business_rules.evidence: Context: 'approval' almost always hides a balance/quota check + multi-level circuit; rules can vary by client/contract/country.

workflow.value: Likely: submitted → pending manager approval → (if manager away/timeout) escalated → approved/rejected. Trigger condition and timing not specified — synchronous check vs. asynchronous timeout.
workflow.evidence: Client states escalation happens 'if manager is away' but not the triggering mechanism.

integrations.value: Notification to manager on submission, notification to escalation approver on trigger — channel and event not specified.
integrations.evidence: Context: 'notify' implies who/when/which channel; not detailed by client.

permissions.value: Undefined whether the escalation approver has full approval rights (same as manager) or a restricted/temporary delegation.
permissions.evidence: Context: permissions are frequently forgotten until acceptance testing; approval circuits are shared across modules.

config_vs_custom.value: Likely client-specific: escalation chain, away-detection rule, and timeout duration probably differ by client/country (labor law), while the base approve/escalate lifecycle is standard.
config_vs_custom.evidence: Context: approval circuits and business rules are configurable per client; French labor law applies to leave.

constraints.value: French labor law (paid leave/RTT) and GDPR on HR data likely apply; may impose minimum notice or record-keeping obligations.
constraints.evidence: Context: regulatory constraint noted for HR domain generally.

edge_cases.value: Not addressed: manager away AND escalation approver also away (chain further?), employee has no manager assigned, concurrent/duplicate escalation, retroactive leave requests, manager returns mid-escalation.

reporting.value: Not stated — likely need at least a history of who approved/escalated and when, for HR traceability.
reporting.evidence: Context: HR data under GDPR; approval trail generally expected but not explicitly demanded here.

risks.value: Risk of double approval (manager and escalatee both act), risk of escalation misfiring if 'away' detection is wrong, rollout risk if it touches shared approval-circuit engine used by other modules.
risks.evidence: Context: approval circuits/permissions are shared across modules — a rule change often ripples.

questions.q: What exactly defines 'the manager is away' — their own approved leave/absence status, an out-of-office flag, a manual delegation they set, or simply no response after N days? And if time-based, what's the timeout?
questions.why: uncertainty x impact: high/high — this decides whether escalation is an automatic, rule-driven trigger (reads another entity's state) or an asynchronous timeout job (queued, retried, visible pending state). Completely different build.

questions.q: Who does the request escalate to — a fixed skip-level manager, HR, or a delegate the manager configures themselves? Does this differ by team/country?
questions.why: uncertainty x impact: high/high — determines the escalation chain's data model (fixed hierarchy vs. configurable delegate) and whether it must vary per client/country.

questions.q: Is there already an approval mechanism for leave in the platform today, or is this the first one? If one exists, what does it currently do?
questions.why: uncertainty x impact: high/high — absence management already exists per platform scope; building on an existing approval circuit vs. creating one from scratch changes effort drastically.

questions.q: Does approving a leave request also require checking the employee's leave balance/quota, and is there already a multi-level circuit (e.g. manager then HR) beyond just manager + escalation?
questions.why: uncertainty x impact: high/high — 'approval' in this platform almost always hides a balance check and multi-level circuit; missing this at build time is a classic gap.

questions.q: Once escalated, does the escalation approver get full approval rights (approve/reject like the original manager), or a limited action? And what happens if the original manager comes back before the escalated approver acts?
questions.why: uncertainty x impact: high/high — permissions are typically forgotten until testing, and this concurrency case (manager returns mid-escalation) determines the conflict-resolution logic needed.

summary.objective: Enable managers to approve employee leave requests, with automatic escalation to another approver when the manager is unavailable, so requests are not stuck waiting on an absent approver.
summary.scope: Likely extends the existing absence/leave module with: an escalation rule (trigger condition and timeout to be defined), an escalation approver role (skip-level manager, HR, or configurable delegate), notifications to the relevant approver at each step, and an audit trail of who approved/escalated a given request. May also need to confirm interaction with existing balance/quota checks and multi-level approval circuits already used elsewhere in the platform.
assumptions: An approval workflow for leave already exists in some form on the platform (absence management is listed as a built feature) and this request extends it rather than creating one from scratch.
The escalation approver is likely a skip-level manager, HR, or a manager-configured delegate — not specified by the client.
The 'manager is away' condition is likely tied to the manager's own recorded leave/absence, but could also be a manual flag or a response timeout.
Escalation rules and timeout durations are probably client/country-configurable rather than hardcoded, consistent with the platform's configurability model.
French labor law and GDPR constraints on HR data apply as they do to the rest of the leave module.
summary.blind_spot: The trigger for escalation is undefined: is 'manager is away' detected automatically from the manager's own leave status (synchronous), or is it a timeout after no response (asynchronous, queued, needs a visible pending state and retry/ordering logic)? These are fundamentally different builds, and the client's phrasing does not indicate which — building the wrong one means redoing the entire trigger and notification logic.
